name: Evergreen Bunching
image_alt: Clusters of green bunching onion shoots
description: A perennial bunching onion that never forms a true bulb — grown entirely for the mild, hollow green tops. Divides into clumps annually and can be harvested continuously for years without replanting.
history: A Japanese heirloom in cultivation for centuries, used throughout East Asian cuisines. Introduced to American gardens by Asian immigrants and adopted widely for its convenience and perennial character.
seasonal_quirks: Truly perennial — mulch clumps in cold winters and they return in spring. Divide clumps every 2–3 years to maintain vigor. Harvest can begin very early in spring before most other crops are established.
pests_diseases: generally more disease-resistant than bulbing onions
harvest_cues: Harvest outer stalks when 8–12 inches tall, leaving the clump center intact to regrow. Cut 1 inch above soil level. Regrows within 2–3 weeks.
variety_notes: One of the most practical plants for a kitchen garden — a permanent bed provides scallions almost year-round in mild climates. Both white and green parts are mild and fully edible.
preservation: Chop and freeze for cooking use. Pickle in rice vinegar.
